title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Question
How strongly do adverse weather conditions explain day-level TTC subway delay burden in Toronto?

title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Goals

Build a reproducible API-based dataset (2014-2026)
Compare regression and classification models
Identify top weather predictors of severe-delay days
Deliver interactive results for exploration


Open with motivation: transit reliability affects daily quality of life. Define severe day as top quartile of delay minutes.

title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Regression

Baseline: Linear Regression
Main model: Random Forest Regressor

title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Classification

Baseline: Logistic Regression
Main model: Random Forest Classifier


Precipitation and weather-stress variables dominate predictive importance.

title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Thank you
Project pages: - Report (report.html / report.pdf) - Interactive visuals (viz.html) - Code + pipeline (scripts/final_pipeline.py)

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
Public transit reliability is a daily quality-of-life issue in Toronto. This project examines how weather conditions are associated with TTC subway delay minutes at the daily level using reproducible data acquisition, feature engineering, regression/classification modeling, and publication-ready outputs.
The specific research questions are:

How strongly are precipitation and weather severity associated with daily total delay minutes?
Do weekday and weekend days have different delay levels once weather is accounted for?
Which engineered weather features are most predictive of daily delay minutes?
Can we accurately classify whether a day is a severe delay day (top quartile of delay minutes)?

The hypotheses are specified in testable null/alternative form:

RQ1 (Weather severity)

H0_1: Precipitation and weather severity have no association with daily total delay (no difference in expected delay across weather groups).
H1_1: Higher precipitation/severity is associated with higher daily total delay.

RQ2 (Weekday vs weekend)

H0_2: Expected daily delay is the same for weekdays and weekends after controlling for weather features.
H1_2: Expected daily delay differs by day type, with higher delay minutes on weekdays.

RQ3 (Predictor relevance)

H0_3: Weather-engineered predictors do not improve predictive fit relative to a minimal baseline.
H1_3: Weather-engineered predictors provide measurable predictive gain.

RQ4 (Severe-day classification)

H0_4: Classification models do not outperform near-random discrimination for severe-day detection.
H1_4: At least one model achieves meaningful discrimination on held-out data (higher ROC-AUC/F1 than baseline).

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
2.1 Model Choice and Rationale
The modeling strategy combines interpretable baselines with non-linear learners:

Linear Regression and Logistic Regression provide transparent baseline relationships for continuous and binary outcomes.
Decision Tree models allow threshold-based interactions (e.g., weather cut points) with straightforward interpretability.
Random Forest models reduce single-tree variance and generally improve out-of-sample stability.
XGBoost adds boosted non-linear capacity for potentially complex interactions between precipitation, temperature, and calendar structure.

This structure tests whether non-linear weather effects improve held-out performance relative to linear baselines.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
2.2 Data Sources
Two data streams were acquired programmatically:

Weather data (Open-Meteo archive API): Daily weather records for Toronto (requested range: 2014-01-01 to 2026-03-31), including precipitation, snowfall, temperature, and wind variables.
TTC subway delay data (Toronto Open Data CKAN API): Incident-level delay records across multiple historical CSV/XLS/XLSX resources from package ttc-subway-delay-data.

All source calls are made inside scripts/final_pipeline.py, and output files are regenerated from scratch when the pipeline is rerun.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
2.3 Data Wrangling and Feature Engineering
The TTC files use inconsistent column names across years. To address this, the pipeline uses conditional parsing rules:

Date-column detection: candidate columns are scored by name pattern (e.g., date, occ date) and empirical parse success rate.
Delay-column detection: candidate columns are scored by delay-like names (e.g., min_delay) and numeric-validity rate, while non-delay text fields are penalized.
Exception handling: each CKAN resource is wrapped in try/except; failed files are logged in outputs/ttc_resource_pull_log.csv rather than stopping the pipeline.

After detection, records are standardized to a common incident-level schema:

date
min_delay
optional line, station (when available)

Records are filtered to valid numeric delay values and aggregated to daily totals:

total_delay_mins (sum of incident delay minutes)
total_incidents (incident count)
median_incident_delay

The final time window is defined by source overlap: weather is requested through 2026-03-31, while TTC records currently extend through 2026-01-31, so the analysis ends on 2026-01-31. A complete daily calendar is then created over this overlap and merged with weather and TTC daily aggregates. Days with no TTC incident records are explicitly retained and coded as:

total_delay_mins = 0
total_incidents = 0
median_incident_delay = 0

The final analysis table contains 4414 calendar days from 2014-01-01 to 2026-01-31, of which 2863 have at least one recorded incident and 1551 have zero recorded incidents.
Engineered predictors include:

mean_temp_c (daily average of max/min temperature)
precip_intensity (precipitation per active precipitation hour)
weather_type (Clear / Light Rain-Snow / Heavy Rain-Snow)
is_weekend_int
calendar features (month_num, day_of_week)

For model training, the numeric feature set is: precip_mm, snowfall_cm, gust_speed, mean_temp_c, precip_intensity, is_weekend_int, and month_num.
The three-level weather severity variable is constructed with explicit breakpoints:

Clear: precip_mm = 0 and snowfall_cm = 0
Light Rain/Snow: precip_mm > 0 or snowfall_cm > 0, but not meeting heavy criteria
Heavy Rain/Snow: precip_mm >= 10 or snowfall_cm >= 5

These thresholds operationalize weather intensity in interpretable categories for both descriptive tables and model features.
For reproducibility checks, data/ttc_incidents_sample.csv stores a random sample of incident records across the full multi-year window rather than a first-year slice.
The classification target is:

severe_day = 1 if daily delay is at or above the 75th percentile threshold (157.0 minutes), else 0.

The 75th-percentile cutoff is used as a pragmatic high-risk threshold for classification; regression results are reported in parallel to avoid over-reliance on a single binary definition of severity.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
2.4 Modeling Strategy
Two modeling tasks were implemented.

2.4.1 Regression
Predicting continuous total_delay_mins on a 70/30 train-test split:

Baseline: Linear Regression
Additional tree models: Decision Tree Regressor, Random Forest Regressor
Complex boosted model: XGBoost Regressor

Key hyperparameters were set before final evaluation (e.g., RF n_estimators=700, max_depth=12; XGBoost n_estimators=400, max_depth=3, learning_rate=0.05).
Metrics: R², RMSE, and MAE on the held-out test set.


2.4.2 Classification
Predicting binary severe_day on a stratified 70/30 split:

Baseline: Logistic Regression (with standardization)
Additional tree models: Decision Tree Classifier, Random Forest Classifier
Complex boosted model: XGBoost Classifier

Key hyperparameters were set before final evaluation (e.g., RF n_estimators=700, max_depth=10; XGBoost n_estimators=700, max_depth=4, learning_rate=0.03, eval_metric=AUC).
Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC on the held-out test set.
Model interpretation includes feature-importance diagnostics.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
3.1 Descriptive Patterns






Figure 1: Figure 1. Monthly aggregated TTC delay minutes (line) and monthly precipitation totals (bars). The long-run pattern shows repeated co-movement during wetter periods.



Figure 1 shows clear long-run co-movement between precipitation load and monthly delay minutes. While seasonality exists, delay peaks are not purely calendar-driven; they align with high precipitation periods.






Figure 2: Figure 2. Distribution of daily delay minutes by weather category. Heavy precipitation/snow days have a higher median and wider tail of extreme delays.



Figure 2 confirms monotonic separation across weather severity categories: heavier weather conditions correspond to higher median delay and greater spread.

Feature-importance outputs (website figure/table) emphasize precipitation-related variables, along with wind and temperature.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
4.1 Hypothesis Assessment

RQ1 / H1_1 supported: Higher precipitation and heavier weather categories are associated with higher daily delay minutes in both descriptive summaries and model behavior.
RQ2 / H1_2 supported: Weekday delay levels remain higher than weekend levels, including within weather-stratified comparisons.
RQ3 / H1_3 partially supported: Weather-engineered predictors provide measurable predictive signal, but gains over linear baselines are moderate rather than dramatic in this daily-resolution setting.
RQ4 / H1_4 supported (with caution): Severe-day classification performs above near-random discrimination on held-out data, though effect size is modest and should be interpreted as risk ranking support rather than deterministic day-level prediction.

title: JSC370 Final Project Report: How Do Weather Conditions Shape Daily TTC Subway Delay Risk in Toronto?
text:
4.2 Limitations

Daily aggregation can hide intra-day dynamics (especially rush-hour clustering).
Historical TTC files are heterogeneous, so column harmonization is robust but imperfect.
Results are predictive/associational rather than causal, and severe-day classification depends on the percentile cutoff.

For interactive exploration of the same dataset, see the Interactive Visualizations page on the project website.

title: JSC370 Final Project: Weather and TTC Subway Delays
text:
This project studies how Toronto weather conditions influence TTC subway delay levels from 2014 to 2026. The Open-Meteo daily weather data were merged with TTC incident-level delay records from Toronto Open Data, and both of the following tasks were evaluated:

Regression models for predicting total daily delay minutes.
Classification models for identifying high-risk severe-delay days.

The analysis is fully reproducible through a single pipeline script that downloads data, performs feature engineering, trains models, and exports publication-ready tables and figures.

title: JSC370 Final Project: Weather and TTC Subway Delays
text:
This project studies how Toronto weather conditions influence TTC subway delay levels from 2014 to 2026. The Open-Meteo daily weather data were merged with TTC incident-level delay records from Toronto Open Data, and both of the following tasks were evaluated:

Regression models for predicting total daily delay minutes.
Classification models for identifying high-risk severe-delay days.

The analysis is fully reproducible through a single pipeline script that downloads data, performs feature engineering, trains models, and exports publication-ready tables and figures.

title: JSC370 Final Project: Weather and TTC Subway Delays
text:
2 Key Findings


2.1 Weather Severity
Heavy rain/snow days show about 1.22x the average delay of clear days.
The precipitation-delay correlation is r = 0.053.


2.2 Day-Type Gap
Weekday average delay minutes are 1.27x weekend levels.


2.3 Model Comparison
Regression RMSE: Linear 106.54, Decision Tree 109.08, Random Forest 105.39, XGBoost 106.98.
Classification ROC-AUC: Logistic 0.614, Decision Tree 0.583, Random Forest 0.598, XGBoost 0.598.


2.4 Risk Threshold
The severe-day threshold is 157.0 minutes/day (75th percentile), flagging 25.1% of days.

5 Data Sources

Open-Meteo archive API: https://archive-api.open-meteo.com/v1/archive
Toronto Open Data CKAN API package: https://ckan0.cf.opendata.inter.prod-toronto.ca/api/3/action/package_show?id=ttc-subway-delay-data

title: JSC370 Final Project: Weather and TTC Subway Delays
text:
6 Reproducibility Note
All outputs are generated by:

Running python3 scripts/final_pipeline.py
Rendering with Quarto (quarto render)

The repository includes:

data/processed_daily.csv (analysis-ready daily panel)
outputs/ (model tables, static figures, interactive figure HTML)
Quarto source files for website, report, and slides

This workflow ensures no hidden local file paths are required.

title: Interactive Visualizations
text: This page contains three interactive visualizations built from the final project dataset, satisfying the Homework 5 requirement.

title: Interactive Visualizations
text:
1 Figure 1: Delay Trend with Precipitation
What this shows: a daily time-series of TTC total delay minutes, with a 30-day moving average and daily precipitation overlay. Why it is useful: it distinguishes isolated spikes from persistent weather-related delay patterns. How to read it: track the blue daily series, then the orange rolling trend, and compare both against teal precipitation bars on the secondary axis.


Caption. Daily TTC delay minutes over time with a 30-day moving average and precipitation overlay.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: 2 Figure 2: Monthly Mean Daily Delay (Year x Month)
text:
2 Figure 2: Monthly Mean Daily Delay (Year x Month)
What this shows: a heatmap of mean daily delay minutes by month and year. Why it is useful: it highlights longer-run seasonal structure and multi-year shifts that are harder to see in raw daily distributions. How to read it: scan darker cells for high-delay month-year combinations, then compare rows (year effects) versus columns (seasonal effects).


Caption. Monthly mean daily delay minutes arranged by year and month.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: 3 Figure 3: Weekday vs Weekend Delay by Weather Type
text:
3 Figure 3: Weekday vs Weekend Delay by Weather Type
What this shows: a weekday-versus-weekend boxplot comparison within each weather category, using incident days only. Why it is useful: it isolates day-type differences without the mechanical pile-up of zero-incident days. How to read it: compare medians and interquartile ranges between weekday and weekend boxes within each weather group.


Caption. Boxplot comparison of daily delay minutes across weather categories by weekday/weekend (incident days only).
